sources.sourceName: Introduction to Sociology, OpenStax 3e (Pressbooks adaptation) - Appendix: Chapter Quiz Answers (odd-numbered)

meta.notes: Sociology (PSBB) items scraped verbatim from OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e Ch. 20 and Ch. 21 section-quiz pages; correct answers from the OpenStax-derived odd-numbered answer key (Pressbooks CC BY 4.0 adaptation). Live OpenStax page shows CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 plus a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing/non-AI per PRD.

questions.stem: The population of the planet doubled in fifty years to reach _______ in 1999?
questions.explanation: The world population reached approximately 6 billion in 1999, having roughly doubled over the preceding fifty years.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 20 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 20 Q1 = A.

questions.stem: What does carrying capacity refer to?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The ability of a community to welcome new immigrants

choices.label: B
choices.text: The capacity for globalism within a given ethnic group

choices.label: C
choices.text: The amount of life that can be supported sustainably in a particular environment

choices.label: D
choices.text: The amount of weight that urban centers can bear if vertical growth is mandated

questions.explanation: Carrying capacity is the amount of life (e.g., population) that an environment can support sustainably given its resources.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 20 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 20 Q3 = C.

choices.label: A
choices.text: That human ingenuity will solve any issues that overpopulation creates

choices.label: B
choices.text: That new diseases will always keep populations stable

choices.label: C
choices.text: That the earth will naturally provide enough for whatever number of humans exist

choices.label: D
choices.text: That the greatest risk is population reduction, not population growth

questions.explanation: Cornucopian theory holds that human ingenuity and technological innovation will solve the problems created by population growth.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 20 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 20 Q5 = A.

questions.stem: What are the prerequisites for the existence of a city?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Good environment with water and a favorable climate

questions.explanation: Cities require a supportive environment, agricultural surplus, and strong social organization, so all of the above are prerequisites.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 20 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 20 Q7 = D.

questions.stem: What led to the creation of the exurbs?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Urban sprawl and crowds moving into the city

choices.label: B
choices.text: The high cost of suburban living

questions.explanation: Exurbs developed as urban sprawl and crowding pushed people to settle in communities beyond the suburbs.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 20 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 20 Q9 = A.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Less affluent residents are pushed into less desirable areas.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Traffic issues, including pollution, become worse.

questions.explanation: Gentrification raises property values and rents, pushing less affluent residents out of newly desirable neighborhoods into less desirable areas.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 20 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 20 Q11 = B.

questions.stem: Urbanization includes the sociological study of what?
questions.explanation: The sociological study of urbanization spans urban economics, politics, and environments, so all of the above applies.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 20 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 20 Q13 = D.

questions.stem: What are ways that human activity impacts the water supply?
questions.explanation: Human activity affects the water supply through sewage, chemical pollution, and radioactive contamination, so all of the above applies.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 20 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 20 Q15 = D.

questions.stem: What is not a negative outcome of shoreline dredging?
questions.explanation: Shoreline dredging damages coral reefs, kills marine life, and ruins sea grass beds; it does not reduce the human population.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 20 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 20 Q17 = D.

questions.stem: Where does a large percentage of e-waste wind up?
questions.explanation: A large share of electronic waste is shipped to and 'recycled' in peripheral (low-income) nations, often under hazardous conditions, an example of environmental injustice.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 20 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 20 Q19 = B.

questions.stem: Which of the following organizations is not an example of a social movement?
questions.explanation: A social movement is organized collective action aimed at social or political change. The NFL is a sports league, not a social movement; the Tea Party, Greenpeace, and NAACP are.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 21 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 21 Q1 = A.

questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of collective behavior?

choices.label: B
choices.text: A group of people interested in hearing an author speak

choices.label: C
choices.text: A class going on a field trip

questions.explanation: Collective behavior involves a relatively spontaneous gathering of people, such as a crowd assembled to hear an author speak, rather than tightly structured groups or individual acts.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 21 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 21 Q3 = B.

questions.stem: According to emergent-norm theory, crowds are:

choices.label: C
choices.text: able to develop their own definition of the situation

questions.explanation: Emergent-norm theory holds that crowds develop their own norms and definition of the situation as members interact, rather than being inherently irrational.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 21 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 21 Q5 = C.

questions.stem: If we divide social movements according to their positions among all social movements in a society, we are using the __________ theory to understand social movements.
questions.explanation: Resource mobilization theory analyzes social movements in terms of their resources and their positions relative to other movements in society.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 21 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 21 Q7 = C.

choices.label: A
choices.text: disruptive and chaotic challenges to the government

choices.label: C
choices.text: the collective action of individuals working together in an attempt to establish new norms beliefs, or values

choices.label: D
choices.text: the singular activities of a collection of groups working to challenge the status quo

questions.explanation: Social movements are organized collective actions in which people work together to establish new norms, beliefs, or values (or to resist change).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 21 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 21 Q9 = C.

questions.stem: If a movement claims that the best way to reverse climate change is to reduce carbon emissions by outlawing privately owned cars, "outlawing cars" is the ________.
questions.explanation: Prognostic framing proposes a solution to the identified problem. Specifying 'outlawing cars' as the remedy is prognostic framing (diagnostic framing identifies the problem).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 21 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 21 Q11 = A.

questions.stem: When sociologists think about technology as an agent of social change, which of the following is not an example?
questions.explanation: Medical advances, the Internet, and genetically engineered food are technological agents of change; population growth is a demographic factor, not a technology.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Introduction to Sociology 3e, Ch. 21 Section Quiz
questions.sourceLicense: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (OpenStax also posts a no-LLM-training notice; SpeedyCAT use is human-facing, non-AI per PRD)
questions.notes: Question + options scraped verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from the OpenStax-derived chapter quiz answer key (Pressbooks adaptation, CC BY 4.0), Ch. 21 Q13 = A.

questions.stem: Core nations that work to propel peripheral nations toward modernization need to be aware of:

choices.label: B
choices.text: preparing for pitfalls that come with modernization

questions.explanation: Core nations promoting modernization must attend to cultural preservation, the pitfalls of modernization, and avoiding hegemonic assumptions, so all of the above applies.
